title: Cream scones (Devon style)
subtitle: Light, plain scones for splitting and eating with clotted cream and jam
excerpt: The plain Devon scone: a simple flour, butter, and cream dough cut into rounds and baked until pale gold. Split and spread: clotted cream first (the Devon order), then jam. Eaten the same day they are baked.
sourceNotes: The cream scone is a cornerstone of the West Country cream tea tradition, with both Devon and Cornwall claiming the definitive version. The distinction is in the layering order: Devon places clotted cream first, then jam; Cornwall reverses this. Mrs Beeton includes a plain scone recipe in Book of Household Management (1861). Eliza Acton documents similar cream-raised scone variations in Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). The recipe here follows the classic Devon approach: cream in the dough, cream on first.

content.text: Scone quality comes down to two things: a light hand and a hot oven.
should be minimal, bring the dough together and cut it without hesitation, then bake immediately in a fully preheated oven.
on first, then jam, is the Devon order.

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, baking powder, and salt into a bowl. Add the cold butter and rub in quickly until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs; some larger lumps are fine. Stir in the sugar. Make a well, add the cream and milk, and cut through with a round-bladed knife until the dough just comes together. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface. Pat the dough to 3 cm thickness. Do not roll. Cut rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Gather the scraps, pat out once more, and cut again.

Place the scones on the lined tray with the sides just touching, this helps them rise straight. Brush the tops with a little cream, avoiding the sides or the scones will rise unevenly. Bake for 13 to 15 minutes until well risen and golden on top. Transfer to a rack and cool for 10 minutes. Serve the same day.
